title: Sales
intro: The test contains descriptions of work situations faced by a sales manager. For each question, choose one of the three answer options. There are no right or wrong answers — answer as honestly and candidly as possible. Completing it will take up to 30 minutes.

questions.text: Do you keep daily statistics on sales volume and the number of meetings in order to track the progress of your work?
Is it hard for you to think about professional growth in sales when many unpleasant things happen in your work?
Do you know everything about the product you sell very well?
Do you strive to improve your sales performance regardless of any unfavorable circumstances?
Do you always work out a plan and scenario for the meeting when preparing for negotiations with potential clients?
Are your personal work results always higher than the performance of your colleagues at the company?
During the closing phase of negotiations, does it happen that you are afraid to ask the client to sign the contract or place the order immediately?
Do you always use the experience of other people's successful actions to improve the quality of your work?
Do you consider it fair, when receiving 100 rubles from a client, to always give them 110 worth of value?
When assessing a situation, is it not so much the facts that matter to you, but rather the opinions of other people?
Does searching for new clients over the phone not cause you the slightest discomfort?
Does the total number of repeat orders from your regular clients never decrease?
Do you always contact the client within three days after the first contact to find out about their willingness to purchase your product?
Do you feel depressed and get into poor shape for no particular reason?
Do you have a very good command of all the specialized knowledge and terminology about the product or service you sell?
Are there moments when you do not fully understand what advantages your product or service offers the client?
During a sale, do you always find it easy to involve the client in the demonstration of the product or service?
Is your sales volume constantly increasing year after year?
Does it often happen that clients' objections regarding the cost of your products or services become a serious obstacle when closing a deal?
Does it happen that you work with lower quality because you poorly understand the client's wishes and needs?
Do you consider it necessary to always know the goals of your company?
Do you experience difficulties when trying to arrange a meeting with a new client?
Does it happen that your clients switch to competitors?
Do you have the feeling that you used to work more successfully than you do now?
Do you agree with the statement that if a client declines a deal, then the sales manager is to blame?
Does the part of a salesperson's work related to keeping documentation, planning, and preparing reports cause you tension?
Do you have situations where you prefer not to deal with difficult clients who promise a lot of trouble when closing a deal?
Can the demands of tiresome clients irritate you?
Are your company's image and reputation always a valuable factor in maintaining the relationship with the client?
Does it happen that you waste time and energy because you did not draw up a clear plan of action in time?
Do clients and colleagues always consider you a good salesperson?
Are there moments in your work when the quality of client service has to be sacrificed?
Due to a high workload, does it happen that you do not manage to contact all of your current clients at least once a month?
Are you considered a very good specialist in technical matters concerning your product or service?
During negotiations with a client, do you sometimes agree to accept "No" as the final answer?
Do you always strive to do more for clients than what they paid for?
Do you often have free time left after you make your calls and hold all your meetings with clients?
Does it happen that you lack the sales tools to work through all of the client's objections and close the deal?
Does it seem to you that most of the time you work under very tense conditions?
Do you always ensure the availability of the necessary amount of promotional materials and presentations to hand out or send to potential clients?
Does it happen that you have doubts about the value of the products or services you sell?
Do you feel uncomfortable when you have to insist on closing a deal?
Are there cases when you do not feel like helping especially demanding and troublesome clients?
Does the lack of experience and knowledge in other people irritate you?
Do you find it difficult to spark interest in a person who was not previously interested in your product or service and was not planning to buy?
Do you prefer to give the client additional time to think instead of insisting on immediately closing the deal?
Do you make sure that the information in the CRM system or client database is always up to date?
In a conversation with a client, can you from time to time make a mistake regarding the products or services you sell?
Before the closing negotiations with a client, do you prepare several versions of the commercial offer in advance?
Due to increased competition in the market, have more of your deals started to fall through than before?
Are you afraid of acquiring a negative reputation as someone who walks over others but insists on closing the deal?
Is the quality of your work sometimes subject to inspection?
Do you believe that ensuring your own security and a stable position is the most important thing at work?
Do you usually enjoy searching for new clients?
Are you always confident that your current clients will remain loyal to the company you work for?
Does it happen that you are upset by the fact that you spend too much time at work?
Do you agree that there is no particular point in spending your time comparing the advantages of your product with those of competitors?
Compared to competitors' products, do you always know the pros and advantages of your product or service very well?
Do your goals and life plans align well with your work in the field of sales?
Are there cases when competitors' arguments and reasoning can catch you off guard?
Are you very satisfied with the current results of your work?
Are you known as a person who, during negotiations with clients, makes every effort to close the deal?
Do you listen to client feedback about your work in order to improve the quality of service?
Does it happen that your clients complain that they were not given the necessary help or received insufficient attention?
Do situations occur in your work where management and the team do not trust each other?
When you find new clients, do most of them show interest in the product or service and are ready to make a purchase?
Do you give clients your personal phone number only in very rare cases?
Do you prefer to plan your meetings with clients in advance, even before the working week begins?
Are there negative situations in your work in which you can become flustered and lose concentration?
Are there aspects concerning your product or service that you do not understand very well?
Do you believe that your sales results depend first and foremost on you yourself?
Does it often happen that during a presentation of a product or service you may not have something necessary at hand to close the deal?
To assess the real level of your competence in sales, is it enough to look at your results?
Do you easily manage to close a deal with at least every third client who meets the necessary requirements?
Have you drawn up a program to improve your qualifications with the various trainings and courses you plan to take?
Do you believe that the main thing in your work is for the client to be completely satisfied?
Do you believe that you should be rewarded not only for actual results, but also for the effort you put in?
Are you considered a person who is very good at finding new clients?
Does it happen that relationships with your current clients can deteriorate?
Do you have the feeling that you are excessively overloaded with work?
Does it seem to you that in life you make many mistakes and often get into unpleasant situations?
When selling a product, do you easily defend its advantages?
Does it happen that you lose confidence in the correctness of your actions?
Do you monitor any changes taking place in the market for your product or service?
Do you often experience difficulties in improving your sales results?
On the way to closing a deal, do client objections arise that you cannot handle?
In your everyday work routine, do you rarely manage to engage in self-development and self-improvement?
Do you help clients even if you get nothing in return?
Are clients sometimes so unreasonable that it is impossible to work with them?
Does it happen that you have difficulties finding potential clients who meet all the necessary requirements?
Do you have situations where you unexpectedly lose clients?
Does it happen that a meeting scheduled with a client did not take place because their plans changed and you knew nothing about it?
Are there moments when the state of your health begins to worry you?
Do you find it difficult to understand the technical description of the product you sell?
Are there moments when you do not feel pride in the company you work for?
Do your deals fall through for reasons that are unclear to you?
In matters concerning sales volume, do you always accomplish the tasks set before you?
Does your manager insistently offer you help in increasing your sales figures?
Is the main thing for you to be useful to the client, regardless of any difficulties?
Do you find it hard to accept another person's point of view, especially if it does not coincide with yours?
Do you prefer to work with emerging new clients rather than with a stable number of regular ones?
Due to a high workload, do you sometimes find it hard to give sufficient attention to all your current clients?
Are you usually little prone to stress and worry in life?
Are there situations when you lack data about the product or service you sell to effectively conduct presentations?
Do you perceive your failures and setbacks as positive and useful experience?
Do you know perfectly well the strengths and weaknesses of all the competitors known to you?
Do you believe that you need training in order to work better?
Do you believe that in a salesperson's work you should do only what you are paid for?
Are most of the potential clients you have found ready for a meeting or a conversation about buying?
Do you agree that current clients can greatly distract you from your main work?
Are there situations when the difficulties associated with the work greatly outweigh the possible positive aspects?
Do you often immerse yourself in studying the specific properties of your product or service?
Does it happen that competition prevents you from doing your job well?
Do you freely control the quality of your own work in order to correct mistakes you have made?
Does it happen that, in order to meet the plan, you are ready to sell a client a completely unsuitable product they do not need?
Is salary the main incentive for you at work?
Do you believe that your ability to increase sales volume has limits?
Are there moments when you fail to effectively handle everyday routine problems at work?
Do you notice that some team members hinder your development within the company?
Do you have a signature way of presenting the product to the client that prevents the most common objections from arising?
